coredata.dc:title: Fluorimetric identification of sulfonamides by carbon dots embedded photonic crystal molecularly imprinted sensor array
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shishun
Shao, Keman
Hong, Chengyi
Chen, Suyan
Lin, Zhengzhong
Huang, Zhiyong
Lai, Zhuzhi
coredata.dc:description: Identification of sulfonamides (SAs) residues in food is vital for human health. A set of 4-channel sensor array was constructed by carbon dots (CDs) embedded in photonic crystal molecularly imprinted (PCMIP@CDs) film which included 3 PCMIP@CDs units and 1 PCNIP@CDs unit to determine typical SAs: sulfadimethoxine, sulfathiazole, sulfaguanidine, sulfamethazine, sulfadiazine. Under the optimal conditions, the response time of the sensor array was only 200 s. Moreover, 300 fluorescence response signals (4 sensor units × 5 sulfonamides × 3 concentrations × 5 repeats) were processed by pattern recognition technique to analyze the ability of the sensor array to recognize 5 kinds of SAs. Subsequently, the linear discrimination analysis (LDA) method was used to identify the five SAs simultaneously with 100 % classification accuracy and the limit of detection was 0.01–0.26 nmol/L. Moreover, the proposed method can effectively identify-five SAs in water and fish samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted
Pattern recognition
Sensor array
Sulfonamides

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of dispersed SiO2 microsphere and PC film
Synthesis of the PCMIP@CDs sensor array
Detection of SAs in fish samples
Statistical analysis of sensor data matrix
Characterization of PC structure and PCMIP@CDs film
Fluorescence sensor array for the cross-responsive discrimination of sulfonamides
Principal component analysis and linear discrimination analysis application
Analysis of mixed standard samples by LDA method

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory of Food Microbiology and Enzyme Engineering of Fujian Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Foundation from the Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21804050 ), the Open Foundation from Key Laboratory of Food Microbiology and Enzyme Engineering of Fujian Province ( Z820224-3 ) and the Cultivation Project in Jimei University for National Foundation ( 202010390021 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of the sensor array and the identification SAs.
SEM images of (A) PC film and (B) PCMIP@CDs film.
Adsorption kinetic curves of each sensor unit (The concentration of SAs 10-6 mol/L).

LDA plots of the sensor array to four groups of SAs (1: SDM, 2: SDM + ST, 3: SDM + ST + SG, 4: SDM + ST + SG + SM2, SAs concentration of 10-6 mol/L, n = 5). Oval outlines indicated groups of SAs at 95 % confidence level.
LDA plots for SAs in (A) aquaculture water and (B) fish samples. Oval outlines indicated groups of SAs at 95% confidence level.
Details about the preparation of each sensor unit.

head.ce:title: Fluorimetric identification of sulfonamides by carbon dots embedded photonic crystal molecularly imprinted sensor array

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Ocean Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Ocean Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Quality Control (QC), WuXi Biologics, 108 Meiliang Road, MaShan Binhu District, Wuxi 214092, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Quality Control (QC), WuXi Biologics, 108 Meiliang Road, MaShan Binhu District, Wuxi 214092, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Identification of sulfonamides (SAs) residues in food is vital for human health. A set of 4-channel sensor array was constructed by carbon dots (CDs) embedded in photonic crystal molecularly imprinted (PCMIP@CDs) film which included 3 PCMIP@CDs units and 1 PCNIP@CDs unit to determine typical SAs: sulfadimethoxine, sulfathiazole, sulfaguanidine, sulfamethazine, sulfadiazine. Under the optimal conditions, the response time of the sensor array was only 200 s. Moreover, 300 fluorescence response signals (4 sensor units × 5 sulfonamides × 3 concentrations × 5 repeats) were processed by pattern recognition technique to analyze the ability of the sensor array to recognize 5 kinds of SAs. Subsequently, the linear discrimination analysis (LDA) method was used to identify the five SAs simultaneously with 100 % classification accuracy and the limit of detection was 0.01–0.26 nmol/L. Moreover, the proposed method can effectively identify-five SAs in water and fish samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, a large number of antibiotic drugs were used to combat bacterial diseases in food. Among them, sulfonamides (SAs) are broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents based on the structure of p-aminobenzenesulfonamide, which are widely used in food because of their broad antimicrobial spectrum, low price, and stable properties (Santos & Ramos, 2016). However, many studies have shown that long-term intake of SAs can lead to health problems such as liver toxicity, kidney failure and intestinal bacterial resistance (Hu, Li, Liu, Fu, Lin & Li, 2022). And the maximum residue standard for most SAs in food tissues is set at 100 µg kg−1, and even some SAs are non-detectable, such as sulfaquinoxaline, sulfaguanidine (SG) and sulfathiazole (ST) (Lu, Cheng, Liu & Cao, 2016). These phenomena have inspired keen research on the rapid and selective detection of SAs. Currently, methods for the detection of multiple SAs in food are mainly based on high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) (Dmitrienko, Kochuk, Apyari, Tolmacheva & Zolotov, 2014). However, this method requires complex laboratory equipment and professional operators, which are expensive and time-consuming. Subsequently, a large number of new techniques are being developed, such as enzyme-linked immunoassays (ELISA) (Li, Cui, Liu, Liu & Wang, 2020), photo-electrochemical sensors (Liu et al., 2021), and Surface Enhanced Raman Scattering (SERS) (Zhou et al., 2021). However, many of the emerging technologies mentioned above are still plagued by high costs or low selectivity and difficulties in characterizing and quantifying all SAs during testing. Therefore, it is essential to develop intelligent sensing identification devices for rapid and specific identification of multiple SAs.

In recent years, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP), as bio-inspired synthetic materials which can selectively bind certain target molecules, have been broadly used in the food analytical field (Villa, Sánchez, Valencia, Ahmed & Gutiérrez, 2021). The prepared MIP sensors commonly used photonic crystals (PCs), fluorescence or chemiluminescent materials as signal sources, which are widely adopted for the detection of antibiotic drugs due to the self-report and high selectivity properties. Wang et al., (2019) fabricated photonic crystal molecularly imprinted polymers (PCMIP) to visualize the detection of oxytetracycline in milk. Jalili, Khataee, Rashidi & Razmjou, (2020) developed a ratiometric/fluorescent sensor that contained different colored carbon dots as dual fluorophores combined with molecularly imprinted polymers for the detection of penicillin-G in milk. Therefore, carbon dots (CDs) and PCs are attractive sensor platforms due to their low cost, simple design and visualization of detection results (Yadav et al., 2021). CDs are a class of zero-dimensional nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm showing excellent photochemical stability and deft synthesis process as a fluorescent probe, which has been widely used in bio-labeling, optical sensing and fluorescent sensor (Sobiech, Luliński, Wieczorek & Marć, 2021; Yuan, Li, Fan, Meng, Fan & Yang, 2016). Moreover, PCs are one photonic material with a certain periodical arrangement pattern of dielectric materials to generate a property called photonic bandgap (PBG) (Cersonsky, Antonaglia, Dice & Glotzer, 2021). According to Bragg’s law, the light with certain wavelengths within the PBG region is prohibited from propagating through PCs, which endows PCs with the structural color and the slow-photon effect (Hou, Li & Song, 2018; Li, Lai, Li & Song, 2019). The characteristics of PC enable the preparation of sensors with brilliant color changes and significant enhancement of fluorescence signal intensity. For example, Eftekhari et al., (2017) reported a fluorescent solid-state sensor with CDs embedded in PCs resulting in a 73-fold amplification of the CDs fluorescence emission, achieving a detection limit of 91 pM for Hg2+ in water. Therefore, the combination of CDs, PC and MIP will enable the visualization as well as trace detection of hazardous substances.

Nevertheless, MIP exhibits specificity at varying degrees for templates and their structural analogs. To compensate for this drawback, chemical sensor arrays based on pattern recognition techniques are proposed. Chemical sensor arrays are a form of sensor with high recognition and accuracy, capable of analyzing and identifying substances with similar chemical structures. The advantage is that despite the low selectivity of single sensor unit for the identification of substances, the identification results still show differentiation and multiple sensor units can be assembled into a sensor with high selectivity and high resolution (Shimizu & Stephenson, 2010). For example, Han et al., (2017) prepared a set of 3-channel sensor arrays labeled with fluorescent dyes to identify 30 types of wine by the difference in fluorescence intensity in age, origin and taste of whiskey, which offers the possibility of identifying counterfeit consumer products (e.g., perfumes and beverages) in the future. Li et al., (2017) reported a set of 4-channel sensor arrays composed of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) with different surface charges, which could rapidly identify 15 microorganisms by the naked eye. It is clear from the above study that chemical sensor arrays can be well applied in food and biological identification.

Pattern recognition is a data processing technique for detecting and identifying the constructed cross-response sensor arrays, mainly used in fields such as face recognition and electronic nose (Granato et al., 2018), and now is gradually used in sensors to improve selectivity and reduce detection errors (Gale & Gunnlaugsson, 2010; Shi, Zhang & Adhikari, 2017). Among them, principal component analysis (PCA) and linear discrimination analysis (LDA) are among the statistical methods commonly used to analyze data in the sensor array. A 4-channel colorimetric sensor array with PCMIP was constructed by Lu et al., (2017) for the differentiation of nitro-aromatics. And the PCA method was utilized to analyze the color data matrix in the sensor array, which induced a great improvement in the discrimination ability of the sensor. Moreover, fluorescent sensor arrays based on six kinds of near-infrared fluorescent dual ligand functionalized Au NPs had been exploited and could analyze various proteins with high precision by the LDA method (Xu et al., 2017). In sensor arrays, the choice of identification signal and conversion method is an essential consideration. The PCA method is an unsupervised dimensionality reduction technique, which can ensure the minimum loss of original data and reduce the dimensionality of the initial high-dimensional linear combination with the help of the matrix operation method, so as to achieve a comprehensive evaluation of the objects under study (Li, Askim & Suslick, 2019). Like PCA, LDA is a dimensionality reduction technique that constructs a set of orthogonal dimensions used to describe the data. Unlike for PCA, however, the output space of LDA is optimized so that the vector connecting the two class-means provides the highest possible separability between these two classes, thus maximizing the signal-to-noise ratio of discriminative power (Askim, Mahmoudi & Suslick, 2013; Li et al., 2019). Thereby, multidimensional data are transformed into low-dimensional data by PCA and LDA methods to obtain visualized statistical analysis plots and are used to distinguish between chemical classes.

Based on the above strategy, we constructed a set of 4-channel sensor array based on CDs embedded in photonic crystal molecularly imprinted (PCMIP@CDs) film for identifying five SAs. This is the first case of PCMIP@CDs array-based fluorimetry for the identification of SAs in fish samples to achieve rapid identification of sulfonamides. The sensor array achieved 100 % accuracy in classifying five SAs in water and fish samples when the LDA method was adopted, which had a basis for the practical application of the sensor array.

Sulfadimethoxine (SDM), sulfathiazole (ST), sulfaguanidine (SG), sulfamethazine (SM2), sulfadiazine (SDZ) and azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) were obtained from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), and methacrylic acid (MAA) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous citric acid, urea, methanol, ethanol and acetic acid were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., ltd. (Guangdong, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and were directly used with no further purification, except for methanol which is chromatographically pure. The water (18.2 MΩ cm) used throughout all experiments was purified with TKA Ultra (Germany).

Glass microscope slides with dimensions 1 × 12 × 20 mm were obtained from Xilong Chemical Co., ltd. (Guangdong, China). Polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) slides were purchased from Hongjunyuan Insulating Rubber Products Co., ltd. (Xiamen, China) and cut into the same dimension as the glass slides.

Fluorescence spectral measurement was performed on a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Reflectance spectra were measured with the MAX2000-Pro fiber optic spectrometer (Wyoptics Co., ltd., China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded with a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan). The PC growth was performed in an incubator (Yiheng Science Instrument Co. ltd., Shanghai, China) at a constant temperature of 40 °C and constant humidity of 60 %.

Carbon dots were prepared by using a facile hydrothermal method (Gao, Lin, Chen, Lai & Huang, 2016). Briefly, 2.1 g of anhydrous citric acid and 1.8 g of urea were sonically dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. Then, the solution was transferred to a stainless-steel Teflon-lined vessel, followed by heating at 200 °C for 4 h in an oven. After the reaction, the vessel was cooled to room temperature. Finally, CDs were dried under vacuum conditions for 72 h, and dissolved in methanol with a concentration of 1 g/L for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dispersed SiO2 microsphere and PC film
ce:section.ce:para: SiO2 particles with a particle size of 368 nm were prepared by the stöber method, as described elsewhere (Stöber, Fink & Bohn, 1968). The prepared silica particles (mass ratio of SiO2 was 0.6 %) were dispersed in ethanol to prepare colloidal crystals on a glass microscope slide, acting as substrate. Then, they were placed in an incubator with a constant temperature of 45 °C and constant humidity of 60 % for 3 days to build a PC film after ethanol evaporation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the PCMIP@CDs sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: PCMIP@CDs were prepared by the thermal polymerization method, using three SAs (SDM, ST and SG) as templates, respectively. The process of preparation was illustrated with ST as an example. About 0.05 mmol of ST and 0.06 mg CDs were dispersed in 1 mL of methanol. The mixture was further added with 0.35 mL of MAA as the monomer and 0.15 mL of EGDMA as the cross-linker under sonication. Next, 0.02 g AIBN as the initiator was added while the pre-polymerization solution was bubbling within a nitrogen atmosphere. Both sides of the PC film were covered with PMMA plates the same size as the PC film to form a sandwich plate. Subsequently, the above solution was absorbed by the sandwich plate through capillary forces until the PC film became transparent. The sandwich plates were placed in a three-neck flask at 70 °C for 5 h for polymerization.

The PCMIP@CDs films were made by soaking the sandwich plates in a 4 % HF solution for 1 h to strip the PMMA plates, followed by a 1 % HF solution for 12 h to completely remove the residual PC framework. Then, the PCMIP@CDs films were immersed in deionized water for 2 h to remove the HF solution. The target molecules were removed with an eluent prepared by a methanol/acetic acid solution (v:v = 16:1), followed by washing with deionized water. The PCMIP@CDs films were immersed in a buffer solution of NaH2PO4/Na2HPO4 (0.01 mol/L, pH 6.0) for further use. SDM PCMIP@CDs and SG PCMIP@CDs were prepared following in the same way as ST PCMIP@CDs, but using SDM and SG as the templates, respectively. Table 1 showed the details of the preparation of each MIP sensor unit. Moreover, the above method was used to prepare PCNIP@CDs, except that without any template molecules in the preparation process.

The prepared PCMIP@CDs and PCNIP@CDs films were respectively placed in a buffer solution of NaH2PO4/Na2HPO4 (0.01 mol/L, pH 6.0) containing different concentrations of SDM, ST, and SG. The fluorescent spectra of the membrane were recorded in a wavelength range of 300–500 nm under excitation at 270 nm with a slit width of 5 nm, and the excitation filter parameter was 250–395 nm, the experiments were repeated five times. The PCMIP@CDs and PCNIP@CDs films were respectively immersed in the SDM, ST, and SG solution to measure the fluorescence spectrum (F). They were immersed in 2 mL phosphate buffer solutions to measure F0 . And the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) was further calculated. Therefore, fluorescence data of the sensor array were collected by the above method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of SAs in fish samples

ce:section.ce:para: The water samples were filtered three times with a 0.22 µm filter membranes to remove impurities. The SDM standard solution was diluted with filtered water to the concentrations of 10-6 mol/L. The PCMIP@CDs membranes were immersed in the corresponding concentration of SAs standard solution at room temperature to be determined the fluorescence spectra, resulting in the fluorescence data matrix. The labeled substances SDM, ST, SG, SM2 and SDZ were numbered 1–5, and the fluorescence intensity data matrix was analyzed by LDA, and compared with the fingerprint of the standard SAs pure substances to verify the accuracy of recognition.

The processed Gadus samples were purchased from a local supermarket in Xiamen City, China. Firstly, approximately 2 g of anhydrous sodium sulfate and 15 mL acetonitrile were added in 5.0 g samples, which were mixed and homogenized at 3000 rpm for 5 min. Next, 15 mL acetonitrile were added to the sample, and the above steps were repeated to transfer the supernatant into a separating liquid funnel. 20 mL of n-hexane was poured into the separating liquid funnel and the solution was mixed thoroughly and stand for 10 min for stratify. The lower layer liquid was collected and transferred in a spin steaming bottle to extract SAs. The liquid was rotated for evaporation at 45 °C until nearly dry. Then, it was dissolved in a 10 mL of phosphate buffer solution. Three standard solutions of 10-6 mol/L SDM (0.1 mL) were added for spiking. The spiked substances SDM, ST, SG, SM2 and SDZ were numbered 1–5 and the detected fluorescence intensity change data matrices were subjected to LDA analysis and compared with the fingerprint profiles of the standard SAs pure substances to verify the accuracy of identification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis of sensor data matrix
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, five parallel fluorescence quenching efficiency data (F0/F) for five SAs at three concentrations (10-6 mol/L, 10-8 mol/L, 10-10 mol/L) were compiled using a sensor array consisting of four sensor units (3 PCMIP@CDs and 1 PCNIP@CDs). When plotting the two-dimensional pattern recognition plots of PCA, the data F0 /F of the obtained sensor array was collected in the software of Origin 2019b as shown in Table S1, S2, and S3. The entered data were analyzed using the Principal Component Analysis Application in Origin to produce two-dimensional pattern recognition plots. Meanwhile, when plotting the two-dimensional pattern recognition plots of LDA, the data in Tables S1, S2, and S3 were input into IBM SPSS Statistics 19, and the scatter plots of the sensor arrays for the six SAs were plotted by the discriminant classification method. The scatter coordinates were subsequently input into Origin2019b to plot confidence intervals using 2D Confidence Ellipse toolkit.

The principle of the proposed approach is shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, three PCMIP@CDs sensor units and one PCNIP@CDs unit were prepared by using sulfadimethoxine (SDM), SG and ST as templates, and the recognition time of the sensor array was only 200 s. There were specific and non-specific adsorptions of each sensor unit to the five SAs, which resulted in differential fluorescence signals of the sensor unit for SAs. The “radar” mode diagrams were used to provide qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis results for five SAs, which further improved the selectivity of each sensor unit. Subsequently, the ability of the sensor array to identify the SAs was analyzed by combining the principal component analysis (PCA) and linear discriminant analysis (LDA) method. The results showed that the LDA method could well analyze the identification ability of the sensor array to five SAs in water and fish samples, which provided a basis for the practical application of the sensor array.

To further investigate the sensing mechanism of PCMIP@CDs membrane, the absorption spectrum of SAs, excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of CDs were measured, and there was no overlap between the UV absorption peak of SAs and the fluorescence spectrum of CDs, as shown in Fig. S1(A). When CDs solution was mixed with SAs solution, there was no obvious fluorescence quenching (Fig. S1(B)). Meanwhile, Fig. S2 showed the fluorescence profiles and reflectance spectra of PCMIP@CDs membranes using SDM as a template sensor unit before and after elution of template molecules. Compared with the fluorescence emission spectrum of PCNIP@CDs (∼380 nm), the fluorescence spectrum of PCMIP@CDs membrane after elution of template molecules (∼370 nm) showed a 10 nm blue shift, which may be related to the change of surface electronegativity of CDs before and after elution (Ma, Zhang, Bai, Huang & Ren, 2019; Zhou et al., 2017). According to the above experimental phenomena, the main form of dynamic fluorescence quenching in PCMIP@CDs membrane is photoinduced electron transfer (PET), in which the lone pair of electrons from the carboxyl group on the surface of CDs of PCMIP@CDs membrane is transferred to the hydrogen bond of SDM when SDM is detected, which leads to the fluorescence quenching of CDs (de Silva, Moody & Wright, 2009). Thus, dynamic fluorescence quenching in the form of PET led to fluorescence quenching of CDs of PCMIP@CDs membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PC structure and PCMIP@CDs film
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the previous study (Lin, Li, Fu, Lai, Peng & Huang, 2020), PC templates were prepared with 368 nm SiO2 microspheres, as shown in Fig. 2 A, the arrangement of SiO2 microspheres in the PC structure showed a periodic three-dimensional faceted cubic structure, which provided a good framework for the preparation of PCMIP@CDs with inverse structure. The pore size of the PCMIP@CDs membrane was about 260 nm with a three-dimensional anti-opal structure and cross-linked pores in Fig. 2B, which had a high specific surface area and was conducive to the mass transfer of substances, thus improving the response speed and sensitivity of the PCMIP@CDs membrane to SAs.

The ratio of template, functional monomer and cross-linker affect parameters such as the three-dimensional pore structure of PCMIP@CDs membrane and the number of imprinted sites (Parlak, Keene, Marais, Curto & Salleo, 2018), the appropriate ratio of template, functional monomer and cross-linker can effectively improve the analysis and identification of sensor arrays, the results were shown in Fig. S3. SG PCMIP@CDs were optimal at the molar ratio of SG to MAA of 1:40, and the signal response of SDM PCMIP@CDs and ST PCMIP@CDs reached the maximum at the molar ratio of SDM, ST to MAA of 1:60 (Fig. S3A), which had the different affinity between SAs and MAA with different chemical structures. The above results showed that the signal difference between each sensor unit reached the maximum when the ratio of SAs to MAAs is 1:60 (Fig. S3B). As shown in Fig. S3C-D, the response values of each sensor unit reached the highest and the maximum differentiation when the molar ratio of MAA to EGDMA was 2.5:1. Therefore, the optimal molar ratio of SAs, MAA and EGDMA was 1:60:24.

Since the number of CDs determines the spatial structure of the imprinted sites and fluorescence intensity of PCMIP@CDs, the inner-filter effect caused by the low or high concentration of CDs will reduce the fluorescence intensity, thus affecting the fluorescence sensing performance of each sensor unit. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence response values of the three sensor units were the highest and the difference between each sensor unit was the highest when the concentration of CDs was 0.06 mg mL−1. In conclusion, the optimization of the preparation conditions resulted in a molar ratio of SAs, MAA, and EGDMA for 1:60:24 and 0.06 mg mL−1 CDs.

Kinetic adsorption experiments were performed on each sensor unit to determine its adsorption equilibrium time, as shown in Fig. 3 . The adsorption kinetics of SDM PCMIP@CDs, ST PCMIP@CDs, and SG PCMIP@CDs were tested at a concentration of 10-6 mol/L SAs, the kinetic curves of each sensor unit were flat when the time reached 200 s. Moreover, the fluorescence signal response values of each sensor unit were inconsistent at each time point. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs is different due to the different chemical structures of SDM, ST and SG, as well as different mole ratios of SAs and MAA in the sensor units prepared.

Individual sensor units were prepared under optimized conditions to investigate the recognition performance of the sensor array on the corresponding template molecules. Three PCMIP@CDs and one PCNIP@CDs membrane were immersed in single SAs corresponding to the template molecules at concentrations of 10-10, 10-8, and 10-6 mol/L, respectively. The intensity change of the fluorescence emission spectra of each sensor unit after the adsorption equilibrium was shown in Fig. S5. As shown in Fig. S5A, the measured fluorescence intensities of SDM PCMIP@CDs were 73.2 %, 54.8 %, and 37.1 % of the initial fluorescence intensities when the SDM PCMIP@CDs were immersed in SDM solutions with concentrations of 10-10, 10-8, and 10-6 mol/L, respectively; the fluorescence signal intensities of ST PCMIP@CDs were 81.4 %, 62.1 %, and 40.2 % of the initial fluorescence intensities (Fig. S5B); the fluorescence signal intensities of SG PCMIP@CDs were 88.7 %, 67.2 %, and 53.2 % of the initial fluorescence intensity (Fig. S5C); and the fluorescence signal intensity of PCNIP@CDs was largely unchanged (Fig. S5D). From the above phenomena, although each sensor unit had an obvious response to the fluorescence signal of the respective target molecules, there was a similar fluorescence quenching signal between each sensor unit, and the phenomenon could lead to false positives during detection. Therefore, it is difficult to obtain accurate qualitative measurements by individual sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensor array for the cross-responsive discrimination of sulfonamides
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the selectivity of the sensor, a fluorimetric sensor array of 4 (4 sensor units) × 5 (5 analytes) × 5 (5 parallel experiments) was constructed by detecting the fluorescence quenching efficiency of different kinds of SAs by each sensor unit (SDM PCMIP@CDs, ST PCMIP@CDs, SG PCMIP@CDs, and PCNIP@CDs). Table S1, S2, and S3 respectively recorded the fluorescence quenching efficiency of SAs with the concentration of 10-6, 10-8, and 10-10 mol/L by four sensor units used to detect, so as to investigate the cross-response characteristics of this sensing array. All four groups of sensor units responded to the five SAs with fluorescent signals which were due to the physical non-specific adsorption ability of the MIP to the targets and the structural analogs of the targets, as evidenced by the response of PCNIP@CDs to five SAs. According to Table S2 and Table S3, each sensor unit had similar fluorescence quenching efficiency for SAs, which made it impossible for the sensor array to perform identification analysis. Therefore, a “radar” mode diagram was introduced to analyze the fluorescence response of each sensor unit to SAs, which can visually describe the cross-response of each sensor unit in SAs solution at different concentrations (10-10, 10-8, 10-6 mol/L).

As shown in Fig. S6, the four sensor units showed different adsorption capacities for SDM, ST, SG, SM2, and SDZ solutions at different concentrations, and each sensor unit had optical cross-response to different SAs compounds. Taking the optical response of SDM PCMIP@CDs to five SAs in Fig. S6A as an example, the area of the pentagon in the “radar” pattern gradually expanded with the increase of SAs concentration, in which SDM PCMIP@CDs showed the largest fluorescence quenching efficiency to SDM and had different fluorescence response to the other four SAs. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S6B, ST PCMIP@CDs had good responsiveness to SDZ in addition to ST, which might be due to the similar chemical spatial structure between ST and SDZ. From the above phenomenon, it can be concluded that the types and concentrations of SAs can be determined by comparing the areas of the pentagonal patterns produced by the four groups of sensor units. Thus, the “radar” pattern recognition technique allowed qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis of the analytes to be measured, and the selectivity of sensor arrays was much higher than that of single sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principal component analysis and linear discrimination analysis application
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, available pattern recognition methods include principal component analysis (PCA), linear discriminant analysis (LDA), hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA), support vector machines (SVMs), and artificial neural networks (ANNs). The above methods were applied to analyze the ability of the sensor array to identify 10-6 mol/L SAs, as shown in Fig. S7. The identification of SAs by PCA and LDA methods which can be well visualized to distinguish SAs were shown in Fig. S7A and S7B. While SAs can only be classified into four categories by HCA method, among which SG and SM2 are undistinguished. And the mapping of ANNs method is more complicated, which is unfavorable for visual identification. Therefore, PCA and LDA were chosen to analyze the ability of the sensor array to discriminate multiple SAs.

PCA and LDA methods were introduced to analyze the fluorescence data matrix to obtain intuitive comprehensive detection results and improve the discriminative ability of the sensor array, resulting in a two-dimensional pattern recognition plot, as shown in Fig. S8, where each point represented a measured value and each ellipse represented the confidence interval of a certain analyte. As an example, the interval range and confidence interval obtained for the detection of 10-6 mol/L SAs were shown in Fig. S8A, and the “ten” line divided the coordinate map into four intervals. The five selected SAs were distributed in the map and the confidence intervals were separated from each other, among which SDM, ST, SG and SM2 were distributed in the four intervals of the coordinate map, while the dispersed distance between SDZ and SM2 and ST was smaller. Therefore, the PCA method was able to discriminate the analysis between SDM, ST, SG and SM2, while could not distinguish SDZ from ST and SM2 at the concentration of 10-6 mol/L. By comparing Fig. S8A with Fig. S8B and Fig. S8C, although the five SAs were distributed in different interval ranges at 10-8 and 10-10 mol/L, their confidence intervals all had a large overlap, thereafter it was difficult to distinguish the SAs at low concentrations by the PCA method. PC1 and PC2 in Fig. S8D showed the weight of the two principal components extracted after the PCA method to the original amount of information, and it indicates that the analysis method is meaningful when the sum of the two exceeds 85 % (Li et al., 2019). At concentrations of 10-10, 10-8, and 10-6 mol/L, the sum of the amount of information possessed by PC1 and PC2 was 75.8 %, 89.1 %, and 90.0 %, respectively. And the characteristic amount of raw information gradually became apparent as the concentration increased. Therefore, the PCA method was able to distinguish the degree of discrimination of the sensor array for the five SAs at high concentrations.

Subsequently, Fisher's LDA was performed to analyze the fluorescence response signal values of the sensor array, and the results were shown in Fig. S9, where LD1 and LD2 were the weight of the two principal components extracted from the original information. At concentrations of 10-8 and 10-6 mol/L SAs, the analytes showed high scattering score on the map and the confidence intervals were separated from each other, while the five SAs had dispersion but partially overlapping confidence intervals under the condition of 10-10 mol/L. At concentrations of 10-10, 10-8 and 10-6 mol/L, the sum of the information content of PC1 and PC2 was 98.4 %, 99.9 % and 97.0 %, respectively (Fig. S9D). The total information content extracted at three concentrations was more than 85 %, and the extracted information content increased as the analyte concentration decreased. Therefore, the LDA method can accurately extract data information from the sensor array at low concentrations and effectively represent the degree of differentiation of the sensor array among the five SAs analytes.

The contributions of the extraction function PCA method and LDA method were compared. The data extraction rate of the LDA method was significantly higher than that of the PCA method at each concentration, meanwhile, the LDA method could maintain a higher data extraction rate at low concentration conditions. The reason why LDA is a supervised technique compared to PCA, which can classify analytes by a linear combination of data. Therefore, the LDA method better represented the performance of the sensor array in distinguishing the five SAs compared to the PCA method, and the data matrix obtained from the sensor array was analyzed by the LDA method in the mixed standard samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of mixed standard samples by LDA method
ce:section.ce:para: In the previous section, sensor arrays had the high discriminatory ability for the five SAs in the plots obtained from the analysis of matrix data using PCA and LDA methods. However, the detection solution was a mixture of multiple SAs in the actual detection, so the discrimination ability of the sensing array for the mixture should be further explored. The experiments in this section were designed for four mixtures of SAs, namely 1:SDM, 2:SDM + ST, 3:SDM + ST + SG, and 4: SDM + ST + SG + SM2, whose concentrations in the solution were all 10-6 mol/L. The experiment was conducted to investigate the ability of the sensor array to discriminate between SDM and ST in the interference environment where SG is present, in the presence of SM2, and the sensor array could discriminate between SDM, ST and SG in the solution system, and the results were shown in Fig. 4 . The mixtures 1–4 were distributed in the four intervals of the coordinate diagram, and the sum of the contributions of LD1 and LD2 was 98.9 % (greater than85 %), indicating that the amount of information extracted can be used to represent the discrimination ability of the sensing array for the four different mixtures. Moreover, the leave-one-out cross-validation of the LDA scores also revealed a classification accuracy of 100 %, as shown in Table S4.

The results of the LDA method of the sensor array for the five SAs spiked in water and fish samples for recovery experiments were shown in Fig. 5 . The solid ovals in the figure represented the confidence intervals of the five spiked SAs, where SDM, SG, ST, SM2, and SDZ were represented by the numbers 1–5, and the dashed ovals represent the standards of five SAs. By comparing the solid ellipses and dashed ellipses in the water samples, the score plot of substances 1–5 was close to or overlapped with the positions of the standards, and the extraction rate of the data matrix by the LDA method was 98.9 % and 95.7 % in water and fish samples, respectively. Therefore, the results of the data can be compared and analyzed with the standards to identify the types and concentrations of SAs contained in samples. Only 2 and 4 of the fish samples had similar score plots with the respective standards, and the rest of the standards deviated from the spiked samples due to the complex composition of the fish, which interfered with sensor arrays. Moreover, the cross-validation of the leave-one-out method of the LDA method showed that the sensor array had 100 % classification accuracy for 5 SAs, and the sensor array is expected to be applied in the development of portable devices for the detection of SAs.

The sensor array was applied to determine the residues of SAs in water and fish samples, and the recoveries of SAs were tested by adding SAs to water and fish samples to investigate the practicality of the method. Meanwhile, the reliability of the method was evaluated compared with the HPLC-UV method. The fluorescence spectra and related assay data were listed in Fig. S10 and Table S5, respectively. The results in Table S5 showed that the recoveries of this method ranged from 81.2 % to 103.0 % with the relative standard deviations less than 10 %. The analytical indexes of the method were close to the reference method, which further confirmed the stability and accuracy of senor array.

The Methods that had been developed for the simultaneous detection of multiple SAs were summarized and the analytical indexes were listed in Table S6 for comparison. It can be seen that the sensor array proposed in this work is able to discriminate between multiple SAs, showing better metrics in the LOD and linear ranges with better analytical precision and accuracy.

In this chapter, CDs were used as the fluorescence source, combined with photonic crystal and molecularly imprinted techniques to construct a set of 4-channel sensor arrays. The sensor array was prepared by using 0.06 mg mL−1 CDs and the molar ratio of 1:60:24 between SAs, MAA and EGDMA to achieve the optimal differentiation capability with a response time of only 200 s. The data matrix of the sensor array for the five SAs was analyzed using “radar” plots and a combination of PCA and LDA methods. The fluorescence signal data matrix of five SAs were analyzed by combining PCA and LDA methods to demonstrate that the sensor array can distinguish five individual SAs or their mixtures at three concentrations. In addition, the sensor array could be used for qualitative and quantitative analysis of residual SAs with 100 % accuracy in water and fish samples. It provides the basis for the preparation of a portable device for the rapid detection of SAs in aquatic products.

Shishun Zhang: Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Keman Shao: Data curation, Investigation. Chengyi Hong: Formal analysis. Suyan Chen: Resources. Zhengzhong Lin: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Zhiyong Huang: Validation, Project administration. Zhuzhi Lai: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Foundation from the Natural Science Foundation of China (21804050), the Open Foundation from Key Laboratory of Food Microbiology and Enzyme Engineering of Fujian Province (Z820224-3) and the Cultivation Project in Jimei University for National Foundation (202010390021).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.135045.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improving SERS sensitivity toward trace sulfonamides: The key role of trade-off interfacial interactions among the target molecules, anions, and cations on the SERS active surface
Analytical Chemistry
